The sons of Japheth Gomer and Magog and Madai and Javan and Tubal and Meshech and Tiras
the sons of Gomer ... Riphath and Togarmah
the sons of Javan Elishah and Tarshish Kittim and Dodanim
the sons of Ham Cush and Mizraim ... and Canaan
the sons of Cush Seba and Havilah ... and Raamah ... Sheba and Dedan
Cush begat Nimrod he began to be
Mizraim begat Ludim and Anamim and Lehabim and Naphtuhim
Pathrusim and Casluhim ... and Caphtorim
Canaan begat ... his firstborn and Heth
the Jebusite and the Amorite and the Girgasite
the Hivite and the Arkite and the Sinite
the Arvadite and the Zemarite and the Hamathite

Uz and Hul and Gether
Arphaxad begat Salah and Salah begat Eber
two sons the name of one was Peleg ... his brother's name was Joktan
Joktan begat Almodad and Sheleph and Hazarmaveth and Jerah
Hadoram and Uzal and Diklah
Obal and Abimael and Sheba
Ophir and Havilah and Jobab all these were the sons of Joktan

name: The generations from Adam
notes: Chronicles opens by reproducing the Genesis line from Adam, the same names set down again as one unbroken record from the first man to the table of nations. Where Genesis 5 spells out each patriarch across several verses, Chronicles names them in a bare list (Adam, Sheth, Enosh, Kenan, Mahalaleel, Jered, Henoch, Methuselah, Lamech, Noah and his sons), so the Genesis verse that names each man is linked to its place in 1Chr 1:1-4. The table of nations is matched the same way, the sons of Japheth, Ham, and Shem appearing in the very same order, with Chronicles compressing several Genesis verses into fewer so that one Chronicles verse may carry the names of two. Genesis adds the narrative detail that the chronicler omits: the lifespans, that Enoch walked with God, the bounds of Canaan, and the closing words that 'by these were the nations divided in the earth after the flood'; that fuller detail is left in place. The shared core is simply the names, the same generations witnessed twice.
